current_fact.fact_text: On April 6, 1814, Napoleon Bonaparte abdicated and was exiled to the island of Elba, marking the nominal beginning of the Bourbon Restoration in France. The man who had redrawn the map of Europe now found his world shrunk to a small Mediterranean island, plotting and reflecting under a very different sky.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

current_fact.match_reason: Napoleon’s exile to a Mediterranean island parallels this coastal, sun-drenched episode—both about being forced into slowness and confronting what remains when the world shrinks.

queue.fact_text: On April 6, 2008, Egyptian textile workers in Mahalla al-Kubra launched a strike over low wages and soaring food prices. Their action inspired urban youth activists to form the April 6 Youth Movement, a group that later played a key role in mobilizing protests during the 2011 Egyptian revolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A grassroots movement challenging economic injustice resonates with an episode that unmasks reggaeton’s roots in marginalized communities and its role as a voice of resistance and identity.

queue.fact_text: On April 6, 1994, the plane carrying Rwandan president Juvénal Habyarimana and Burundian president Cyprien Ntaryamira was shot down near Kigali. Within hours, extremist militias launched the Rwandan genocide, a meticulously organized campaign that would kill hundreds of thousands of Tutsi and moderate Hutu in about 100 days.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Confronting a genocide requires space for grief and raw emotion; Flamenco’s history of channeling deep collective pain and resilience makes this episode a fitting emotional mirror.

queue.fact_text: On April 6, 1958, Capital Airlines Flight 67 crashed near Freeland, Michigan, while attempting to land in heavy icing conditions, killing all 47 people on board. The accident highlighted how invisible weather phenomena can sabotage advanced machinery and contributed to better understanding of in‑flight icing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #15 - Unfreezing
episode.description: A short, honest Morning Drop with no grand theme — just four tracks, a bit of voice, and the decision to not wait for the 'right' moment. Room to breathe today.
songs: Jane Birkin & Serge Gainsbourg - Je t’aime… moi non plus
Bernard Badie - In Love (DJ Quad's at Night Mix)

queue.match_reason: A crash caused by ice literally freezing an aircraft pairs symbolically with an episode about ‘unfreezing’—taking small honest steps instead of waiting for perfect conditions.

queue.fact_text: On April 6, 1930, at the end of his 24‑day Salt March, Mahatma Gandhi bent down on an Indian beach, picked up a lump of salty mud, and declared, “With this, I am shaking the foundations of the British Empire.” By turning the simple act of making salt into civil disobedience, he transformed everyday materials into instruments of mass, nonviolent resistance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: Gandhi’s nonviolent defiance against empire aligns with this episode’s focus on music as protest, where riffs and lyrics, like salt, become tools to shake powerful systems.

queue.fact_text: On April 6, 1974, just as ABBA were conquering Eurovision in Brighton, another wave of sound was forming: the California Jam’s roaring amplifiers foreshadowed the arena rock spectacles that would dominate live music for years. It was a day when pop polish and distorted guitars shared the same date stamp in music history.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A landmark day for big, bold pop and rock beginnings fits with Sedna’s very first episode—a mixed ‘bomb’ of sounds marking the start of its own musical universe.

queue.fact_text: In the same 1909 expedition that claimed the North Pole, Matthew Henson—once a cabin boy and the son of formerly enslaved parents—served as Peary’s indispensable right hand. Fluent in Inuktitut and trusted by Inuit guides, Henson left footprints in the snow that history took decades to fully recognize.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: Henson’s overlooked role and deep connection with Arctic Indigenous communities echo this episode’s focus on desert peoples, exile, and landscapes that quietly shape unsung heroes.

queue.fact_text: The April 6 Youth Movement in Egypt chose its name to remember the 2008 Mahalla strike, but it also became a symbol of digital-era activism. By the late 2000s, they were using Facebook, SMS, and blogs as organizational tools, foreshadowing how social media would later amplify uprisings around the world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: Just as Auto‑Tune quietly reshaped how voices are heard, social media quietly reshaped how dissent is organized; both show tools shifting power and sparking debate about authenticity.

queue.fact_text: When Early Bird went operational in 1965, it transmitted the first live TV images between North America and Europe without relying on short-lived relay balloons. One charming demonstration broadcast included a split-screen view of a U.S. and European clock side by side, letting viewers literally watch time zones meet in real time.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A satellite linking continents in real time fits an ‘evening flow’ that drifts across borders and time zones, creating one shared audio space for listeners scattered around Earth—and beyond.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1973, NASA launched Pioneer 11, a small, resilient spacecraft that went on to become the first probe to fly past Saturn. After braving intense radiation near Jupiter, it threaded a path through Saturn’s rings, sending back haunting images and data that expanded our understanding of the outer Solar System.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spacecraft pushing into the outer Solar System echoes the episode’s theme of treating emptiness as creative space and making a bold ‘space shift’ in perspective.

published.fact_text: In 1965, the satellite nicknamed Early Bird (Intelsat I) became the first commercial communications satellite placed in geosynchronous orbit. For the first time, live television, phone calls, and data could quietly bounce over the Atlantic almost continuously, shrinking oceans into milliseconds.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Early Bird quietly launched our era of constant connectivity, which this episode examines from the inside through our dependence on networks and devices.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1974, an unknown Swedish group called ABBA won the Eurovision Song Contest in Brighton with “Waterloo.” That three-minute performance turned a local pop act into a global phenomenon and marked the beginning of Sweden’s long-running dominance in pop songwriting.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

published.match_reason: ABBA’s Eurovision breakthrough helped set the stage for Europe’s synth-driven pop and club culture, which this episode celebrates through 80s dance-floor legends.

published.fact_text: Also in 1974, over 200,000 people gathered at Ontario Motor Speedway in California for the first California Jam festival. The one‑day rock marathon, featuring bands like Deep Purple and Emerson, Lake & Palmer, set records for sound volume, attendance, and revenue, capturing rock at its most excessive and electrifying.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The raw, amplified power of California Jam mirrors this episode’s high-voltage guitars and rock energy designed to jolt listeners awake.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1909, Robert Peary and Matthew Henson claimed to have reached the North Pole after a brutal Arctic expedition over shifting ice and in lethal cold. Henson, an African American explorer and master navigator, long went under-credited, and Peary’s exact route remains disputed, making this a story where heroism and uncertainty coexist on the edge of the world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the North Pole expedition and this episode are anchored in the mystique and danger of polar regions, mixing cold extremes with quiet, reflective journeys.

published.fact_text: In 1580, the Dover Straits between England and France shuddered with one of the largest recorded earthquakes in the region’s history. Medieval and early modern observers described church bells ringing on their own and masonry crumbling, long before plate tectonics would explain the invisible forces underneath.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description: In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

published.match_reason: An unexpected earthquake mirrors the episode’s themes of sudden upheaval and weathering storms, with songs about rain and resilience following the tremors of change.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1812, the Duke of Wellington’s forces stormed the fortress city of Badajoz during the Peninsular War. The brutal assault broke a key French stronghold but was followed by days of chaotic looting and violence, a stark reminder that even ‘turning points’ in war can leave deep human scars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A violent siege in a war of liberation pairs with Rage Against the Machine’s fiercely political sound, both confronting the costs and anger tied to conflict and power.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 2009, a magnitude 6.3 earthquake struck near L’Aquila in central Italy, collapsing historic buildings and killing 307 people. In the aftermath, controversy erupted when several scientists were controversially prosecuted over alleged failures in risk communication, raising difficult questions about how societies handle uncertainty in science.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: The tragic quake and the debates around scientific responsibility resonate with this episode’s focus on how learning, error, and communication shape our understanding of risk and progress.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1776, during the American Revolutionary War, ships of the fledgling Continental Navy failed to capture a British dispatch boat near Block Island. Though a minor action, it shows how nations are often born through trial-and-error skirmishes at sea long before they win decisive battles on land.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A small naval clash in choppy Atlantic waters pairs naturally with an episode recorded aboard a boat, both centered on life, risk, and story-telling at sea.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1941, Nazi Germany simultaneously launched Operation 25 and Operation Marita, invading Yugoslavia and Greece. The swift campaigns redrew the map of southeastern Europe, but also sparked fierce resistance movements whose cultural and musical echoes would last for decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: The invasion carved through Balkan and Greek landscapes, while this episode journeys musically through nearby Mediterranean mountains, hinting at how history and geography shape the stories people later sing about.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 46 BC, Julius Caesar defeated the forces of Metellus Scipio and Cato the Younger at the Battle of Thapsus in North Africa. The victory helped Caesar dismantle the last major pockets of Republican resistance, nudging Rome toward empire and raising enduring questions about power, principle, and civil war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: A pivotal Roman civil-war battle that reshaped Italy’s future pairs with an episode steeped in Italian voices, balancing foggy introspection and fiery southern energy—much like Rome’s own tensions between order and upheaval.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1998, Pakistan test‑fired medium‑range missiles capable of reaching deep into India, signaling an escalating nuclear-capable arms race in South Asia. The tests underscored how rocketry and nuclear physics can be used not just to explore space but to project power across borders.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: Missile tests show the double edge of advanced technology, while this episode imagines technology as a vehicle for peaceful voyages through sound and space, not for escalation.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1985, a bloodless coup d’état led by Field Marshal Abdel Rahman Swar al-Dahab removed Sudanese president Gaafar Nimeiry from power. Remarkably, the new leader later ceded authority to a civilian government, making this a rare example of a military ruler who voluntarily stepped away.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A political turning point in an African nation pairs with an episode dedicated to Afrobeat, a genre deeply intertwined with African politics, protest, and post-colonial identity.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1945, Yugoslav Partisan forces liberated Sarajevo from German and Croatian control near the end of World War II. The city, later known for its multicultural layers and tragic 1990s siege, briefly glimpsed a post‑war future of rebuilding and shared identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: Sarajevo’s liberation and later history invite reflection on recovery and transition, themes mirrored in this introspective episode about healing, looking back, and slowly moving forward.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1936, a devastating tornado struck Gainesville, Georgia, as part of the Tupelo–Gainesville tornado outbreak, killing 203 people in the town alone. It remains one of the deadliest tornado disasters in U.S. history, with entire city blocks reduced to splinters in minutes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: The violent chaos of a tornado makes the episode’s gentle, slow-motion atmosphere feel like a needed counterbalance—a sonic shelter after nature’s sudden fury.

published.fact_text: On April 6, 1814, Napoleon Bonaparte abdicated and was exiled to the island of Elba, marking the nominal beginning of the Bourbon Restoration in France. The man who had redrawn the map of Europe now found his world shrunk to a small Mediterranean island, plotting and reflecting under a very different sky.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: Napoleon’s exile to a Mediterranean island parallels this coastal, sun-drenched episode—both about being forced into slowness and confronting what remains when the world shrinks.
